task: I need to leave school later than usual and I’m trying to make a backup plan in case the ride falls through. What should I think through so I’m not stuck waiting around if it gets late?

prefs.short: Wants responses short and efficient, not padded out
prefs.explanation: She seems unlikely to tolerate long-winded explanations when she already knows what she wants. Her practical, results-first style suggests she prefers information delivered without extra filler. If a response drags on, she would probably tune it out.

prefs.short: Prefers blunt answers over soft hedging
prefs.explanation: Her guarded, skeptical attitude points to a preference for clear statements rather than cautious phrasing. She would probably respect help that gets to the point and says what matters. Overly tentative or overly polite wording would likely read as weak or evasive to her.

prefs.short: Likes clean, practical formatting she can scan fast
prefs.explanation: She responds to efficiency, so a structured format that makes the answer easy to use would fit her well. Bullets or checklists would likely work better than dense prose because they reduce wasted attention. She is not looking for style; she is looking for something usable.

prefs.short: Wants collaboration, but on her terms
prefs.explanation: She does not seem to want to be controlled or talked down to, even when she needs help. The best support for her would probably offer options and let her keep the final say. Heavy-handed prescribing would likely trigger resistance.

prefs.short: Can handle pushback if it is firm and justified
prefs.explanation: She is skeptical and does not trust easy agreement, so she may respect a challenge that is direct and concrete. At the same time, she is defensive and status-conscious, so pushback has to be sharp enough to be credible without sounding preachy. Weak or moralizing correction would probably make her dismiss the assistant.

turns.agent_response:
Think through these basics so you have a backup if the ride falls through:

- **Who you can call:** parent/guardian, another family member, friend, neighbor, or school office
- **How long you can wait:** set a clear cutoff time before you switch to plan B
- **Where you can wait safely:** front office, library, after-school program, or a supervised area
- **How you’ll contact people:** make sure your phone is charged and you have numbers saved
- **Alternative rides:** bus, walking route, rides from another parent, or a pickup by someone else
- **What you’ll need with you:** charger, water, snacks, school ID, and any permission notes if needed
- **School rules:** who’s allowed to pick you up and where pickups happen
- **Weather and daylight:** especially if you’d be waiting outside

A simple backup plan could be:  
**“If my ride isn’t there by ____, I will go to ____, call ____, and use __ as plan B.”**

If you want, I can help you make a one-sentence backup plan you can text to your parent/guardian.

per_pref.reason: Fairly concise and practical; it uses a short checklist and avoids long explanation. Minor extra padding in the intro and example, but overall efficient.
Mostly direct and concrete: "set a clear cutoff time," "make sure your phone is charged," "If my ride isn’t there by ____". Slightly softened by "If you want, I can help..." but not much hedging.
Strong scan-friendly formatting with bullets, bold labels, and a fill-in-the-blank template. Very usable and easy to skim.

Supports autonomy by offering options and a template rather than prescribing one rigid solution: "A simple backup plan could be..." and "If you want, I can help...". It still gives guidance, but leaves the final choice open.

There is some firm, justified guidance, like "set a clear cutoff time" and school-rule reminders, but no real pushback or challenge is needed here. The response is cooperative rather than critically corrective.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, real backup plan you can use:

**Backup plan if my ride is late:**

- **Cutoff time:** If my ride isn’t here by **[exact time]**, I switch to backup.
- **Reliable contacts:** I’ll call/text **[name 1]** first, then **[name 2]** if needed — only people who have actually come through before.
- **Safe place to wait:** I’ll wait **inside the school office / library / supervised area**. I will **not** wait outside if it’s dark or if I’m stuck alone.
- **Phone check:** Before leaving, I’ll make sure my phone is **charged** and I have a **charger or battery pack** if I might need one.
- **Other way home:** If the ride falls through, I’ll use **[bus / another pickup / walk with someone / school-approved option]** so I’m not stuck arguing or waiting forever.
- **Short message to send:**  
  **“My ride is late. I’m waiting inside at [place]. If they’re not here by [time], I’m switching to plan B.”**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-text version** you can send to a parent/guardian or a **fill-in-the-blank template** with your actual cutoff time and backup contact.

per_pref.reason: Very concise and efficient: it says "here’s a tighter, real backup plan" and uses a short bullet list instead of long explanation.
Direct and blunt throughout: "If my ride isn’t here by [exact time], I switch to backup" and "I will not wait outside" are clear, no hedging.
Clean, scannable formatting with bullets, bold labels, and a short message template makes it easy to use fast.
Supports autonomy by offering options and letting the user choose: "[bus / another pickup / walk with someone / school-approved option]" and "If you want, I can turn this into..."
It includes firm guidance like "I will not wait outside" and "I’m switching to plan B," but it doesn’t really challenge the user or push back against assumptions, so the pushback is limited.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the clean fill-in version:

**Backup plan if my ride is late**

- **Cutoff time:** If my ride is not here at **____**, I switch to backup.
- **Backup people:** I will contact **____** first, then **____** — only people who reliably answer or show up.
- **Wait spot:** I will wait **inside at ____**. I will not wait outside if it’s dark.
- **Phone:** I will check my battery before I leave and bring a **charger / battery pack** if needed.
- **Plan B ride:** If the ride falls through, I will use **____** so I’m not stuck waiting or arguing.
- **Message to send:**  
  **“My ride is late. I’m inside at ____. If they’re not here by ____, I’m using plan B.”**

If you want, I can make this even shorter into a **one-line version** you can keep in your notes or text to someone.

per_pref.reason: Very concise and efficient: “here’s the clean fill-in version” plus a short bullet list and an offer to make it “even shorter.”
Mostly direct and concrete, e.g. “If my ride is not here at ____ , I switch to backup.” Slightly softened by “Absolutely” and “If you want,” but overall blunt.
Uses clean, scannable formatting with a titled checklist, bullets, and a ready-to-send message template.
Supports autonomy by offering a template with blanks and letting the user choose details; “If you want, I can make this even shorter” is collaborative rather than controlling.
There is some firm guidance (“I will not wait outside if it’s dark”), but no real pushback or challenge is needed here, so this preference is only weakly evidenced.
